machine-learning.plain: Getting a computer to work out the rule from examples, instead of being handed the rule.
machine-learning.definition: A family of methods where a program improves at a task by adjusting itself based on data, rather than following instructions a person wrote for every case. "Learning" means: change the settings until the outputs match the examples better.

model.plain: The rule-of-thumb machine we're building — settings plus a recipe that turns an input into a prediction.
model.definition: A mathematical function with adjustable settings (parameters) that maps inputs to outputs. A straight line with its slope and offset is a model; so is a billion-parameter neural network. "Training a model" means tuning its parameters.

training.plain: The guess-grade-adjust loop: try settings, measure how wrong the guesses are, nudge the settings, repeat.
training.definition: The process of adjusting a model's parameters to reduce its loss on a set of examples. Most commonly done with gradient descent.

data-point.plain: One example: one row in your notebook, one dot on the chart.
data-point.definition: A single recorded case, usually an input (or several) together with the answer we'd like the model to produce for it. A dataset is a collection of data points.

feature.plain: One of the things we know about each example — a column in the notebook, like the day's temperature.
feature.definition: An individual measurable property used as input to a model. Real problems usually have many features per data point; choosing and preparing them well (feature engineering) often matters more than the choice of model.

label.plain: The right answer for an example — what actually happened, like that day's real sales.
label.definition: The known output value attached to a data point during training. The model's predictions are graded against the labels.

prediction.plain: The model's guess for an input it's given.
prediction.definition: The output a model produces for a given input. Written ŷ ("y-hat") to distinguish the guess from the true answer y.

parameter.plain: A setting inside the model that training is allowed to change — a knob the learning turns.
parameter.definition: A value the training process adjusts to fit the data, such as a line's slope and offset, or a neural network's millions of weights. Distinct from hyperparameters, which are set by the human before training starts.

weight.plain: How much an input matters to the prediction. For a line on a chart, it's the slope.
weight.definition: A parameter that multiplies an input. A large weight means that input strongly influences the output; zero means it's ignored; negative means it pushes the output the other way.

bias.plain: The model's starting offset: the prediction when every input is zero. For a line, where it crosses the axis.
bias.definition: A parameter added to the weighted inputs, letting the model's output start somewhere other than zero. (Unrelated to "bias" meaning unfairness in data — that's a different concept that shares the word.)

loss.plain: The model's grade: how wrong its guesses are, boiled down to one number. Lower is better, like golf.
loss.definition: A function that measures the gap between predictions and true answers across the data. Training is the search for parameter values that make the loss small. Different tasks use different losses; mean squared error is the classic one for regression.

mean-squared-error.plain: Grade a model by taking each miss, squaring it, and averaging: misses in both directions count, and big misses count extra.
mean-squared-error.definition: A loss for regression: the average of (true value − prediction)² over all data points. Squaring keeps misses above and below from cancelling out and punishes large errors disproportionately — which is also why outliers pull hard on the fit.

residual.plain: One example's miss: the gap between the real answer and the model's guess — the little vertical bar from a dot down to the line.
residual.definition: The difference y − ŷ for a single data point. Patterns in the residuals (rather than random scatter) are a sign the model is missing structure in the data.

outlier.plain: A weird dot far from all the others — a typo, a fluke, or a genuinely unusual case.
outlier.definition: A data point that lies unusually far from the overall pattern. Squared-error losses give outliers outsized influence, so handling them (investigating, capping, or using a robust loss) is a routine part of real ML work.

gradient.plain: The "which way is uphill" arrow: for each setting, does the loss go up or down if I nudge it, and how steeply?
gradient.definition: The collection of a function's derivatives with respect to each parameter — the direction of steepest increase. Training moves parameters against the gradient (downhill) to shrink the loss.

derivative.plain: How fast one thing changes when you nudge another — the mathematical answer to "if I turn this knob a tiny bit, what happens?"
derivative.definition: The rate of change of a function at a point. The partial derivative ∂L/∂w reads "how the loss L changes as the weight w changes, holding everything else still." It is the core tool that lets training know which way to adjust each parameter.

gradient-descent.plain: Learning by walking downhill: repeatedly nudge every setting in whichever direction makes the grade a little better.
gradient-descent.definition: An algorithm that iteratively updates parameters a small step against the gradient of the loss: w ← w − η·∂L/∂w. Almost all of deep learning is powered by variants of this loop (SGD, momentum, Adam).

learning-rate.plain: How big each nudge is. Too small and learning crawls; too big and it overshoots and bounces around.
learning-rate.definition: A hyperparameter multiplying the gradient in each update step. One of the first knobs to tune when training misbehaves.

linear-regression.plain: Fitting the best straight line through your dots, then using the line to predict.
linear-regression.definition: A model whose prediction is a weighted sum of the inputs plus a bias: ŷ = wx + b (one input) or ŷ = w₁x₁ + … + wₙxₙ + b (many). Usually fit by minimizing mean squared error — which has a closed-form solution, but is also the standard first example of gradient descent.

regression.plain: Predicting a number: tomorrow's temperature, a house price, how many ice creams.
regression.definition: The family of tasks where the output is a continuous quantity. Contrast with classification, where the output is a category.

classification.plain: Predicting a category: spam or not, cat or dog, which digit is in the picture.
classification.definition: The family of tasks where the output is one of a fixed set of classes. Uses different losses and metrics than regression (accuracy, precision/recall, cross-entropy).

supervised-learning.plain: Learning from worked examples: every practice question comes with the right answer attached.
supervised-learning.definition: Training on data points that include labels, so the model's guesses can be graded directly. Regression and classification are both supervised.

unsupervised-learning.plain: Learning without answer keys: find structure in the data on your own, like sorting a pile of photos into groups.
unsupervised-learning.definition: Methods that work on unlabeled data — clustering (k-means), dimensionality reduction (PCA), density estimation. The model discovers patterns rather than matching known answers.

training-set.plain: The examples the model practices on — the homework.
training-set.definition: The portion of the data used to fit the parameters. Performance here is not proof of learning: a model can ace data it memorized.

test-set.plain: Examples held back and never shown during practice — the real exam.
test-set.definition: Data kept aside to estimate how the model performs on cases it hasn't seen. Looking at it during development erodes its value; that's what a separate validation set is for.

overfitting.plain: Memorizing the homework instead of learning the subject: perfect on practice examples, lost on new ones.
overfitting.definition: When a model captures noise and quirks of the training set rather than the underlying pattern, so training loss keeps improving while test performance gets worse. The central occupational disease of ML; regularization, more data, and simpler models are the treatments.

underfitting.plain: The model is too simple to catch the pattern at all — a straight line through a clearly curved trend.
underfitting.definition: When a model lacks the capacity (or training) to capture the real structure, performing poorly even on the training set. The cure is a richer model, better features, or longer training.

hyperparameter.plain: A setting the human picks before training starts — training tunes the parameters, you tune the hyperparameters.
hyperparameter.definition: Configuration values not learned from data: learning rate, model size, number of training steps, regularization strength. Chosen by experiment, usually judged on a validation set.

neural-network.plain: Many simple weighted-sum units stacked in layers, with gentle bends between them — enough to learn shapes no straight line can.
neural-network.definition: A model built from layers of linear transformations (weights and biases) separated by nonlinear activation functions. Trained end-to-end with gradient descent via backpropagation. The foundation of deep learning.

vector.plain: A list of numbers — which is also an arrow: [3, 1] is "walk 3 east, 1 north."
vector.definition: An ordered list of numbers, viewable geometrically as a point or arrow in space with one dimension per entry. In ML, every data point is a vector (one entry per feature), and so are a model's parameters and gradients.

matrix.plain: A table of numbers — or equally, a stack of vectors, one row per data point.
matrix.definition: A rectangular grid of numbers with rows and columns. A dataset of n examples with d features is an n×d matrix; a neural network layer's weights form a matrix that transforms one vector into another.

dot-product.plain: Multiply two lists position by position and add it all up: one number that measures how much two directions agree.
dot-product.definition: For equal-length vectors a and b: Σᵢ aᵢbᵢ. Positive when they point similar ways, zero at right angles, negative when opposed. The core operation inside linear models and every neural network layer.

mean.plain: The plain average: add everything up, divide by how many.
mean.definition: The sum of values divided by their count; the center of mass of a distribution. Written μ for a population or x̄ ("x-bar") for a sample.

variance.plain: The average of the squared gaps from the mean — spread, measured in squared units.
variance.definition: σ²: the mean of (value − mean)² over the data. Its square root is the standard deviation, which lives back in ordinary units. Note the family resemblance to mean squared error — MSE is the variance of the misses.

standard-deviation.plain: How far values typically stray from their average — tight huddle or wide scatter, as one number.
standard-deviation.definition: The square root of the variance. For roughly bell-shaped data, ~68% of values fall within one σ of the mean and ~95% within two.

distribution.plain: The shape of a pile of values: where they cluster, how far they spread, whether they lean.
distribution.definition: A description of how likely each possible value is. Summarized by statistics like the mean and standard deviation; visualized as a histogram or density curve.

normal-distribution.plain: The bell curve: values pile up symmetrically around the average and thin out fast on both sides.
normal-distribution.definition: The distribution that arises whenever many small independent effects add up — which is why measurement noise so often follows it. Fully described by just μ and σ. The 68/95 rule of thumb comes from its shape.

validation-set.plain: A middle pile of data for repeated tune-and-check cycles — so the real test set stays untouched until the very end.
validation-set.definition: Data held out from training and used to compare model variants and tune hyperparameters. Because you iterate against it, it slowly gets "used up" too — which is why a final, once-only test set is kept separate.

generalization.plain: Doing well on new examples, not just the ones you practiced on. The entire point of learning.
generalization.definition: A model's performance on data drawn from the same source but not seen during training. Estimated by the test-set grade; the train-vs-test gap measures how much of the training performance was memorization.

regularization.plain: A fee for wildness: penalize dramatic settings so the model only bends where the data genuinely pays for it.
regularization.definition: Any technique that discourages overfitting by constraining a model's effective flexibility — most classically by adding a penalty term (scaled by λ) on parameter size to the loss. Weight decay, dropout, and early stopping are all regularizers in spirit.

threshold.plain: Where you draw the line on a model's score: above → flag it, below → let it pass.
threshold.definition: The score above which a classifier's output is treated as positive. Moving it trades false alarms against misses (precision against recall); the right setting depends on which mistake costs more, not on the model.

accuracy.plain: The share of cases handled correctly — and nearly meaningless when the thing you're hunting is rare.
accuracy.definition: (correct predictions) / (all predictions). With imbalanced classes it mostly reflects the imbalance: "never fraud" is 99.9% accurate if fraud is 1-in-1,000. Always read it alongside precision and recall.

precision.plain: When you point, are you right? Of everything flagged, the share that truly was the thing.
precision.definition: TP / (TP + FP): among positive predictions, the fraction that are actually positive. Low precision means crying wolf. Raise the threshold and precision typically rises — at recall's expense.

recall.plain: Of what's really out there, how much did you find? The share of actual cases you caught.
recall.definition: TP / (TP + FN): among actual positives, the fraction the model flagged. Low recall means wolves stroll past. The king metric whenever a miss is the catastrophic error (screening, fraud, safety).

false-positive.plain: A false alarm: the model flagged something that was actually fine.
false-positive.definition: A negative case predicted as positive — a real email junked, a healthy patient flagged. Its cost relative to the false negative's should drive where you set the threshold.

false-negative.plain: A miss: the thing was really there, and the model let it slip past.
false-negative.definition: A positive case predicted as negative — spam reaching the inbox, a disease left undetected. When this is the catastrophic error, optimize for recall and accept more false alarms.

confusion-matrix.plain: The 2×2 scorecard: caught, correctly passed, false alarms, and misses — all four counts visible at once.
confusion-matrix.definition: A table of prediction vs. reality counts (TP, FP, TN, FN for two classes; N×N for more). Every headline metric — accuracy, precision, recall — is a ratio of cells from this table, which is why it's the honest place to start.

logistic-regression.plain: Linear regression's yes/no cousin: score with the weighted sum, then squash the score into a probability.
logistic-regression.definition: A classifier computing σ(w·x + b): a linear score passed through the sigmoid, trained with log loss. Its decision boundary is a straight line (hyperplane). Despite the name, it does classification — still the standard first model for yes/no problems.

sigmoid.plain: The S-shaped squasher: any number in, something between 0 and 1 out — big scores land near 1, big negatives near 0.
sigmoid.definition: σ(z) = 1/(1+e⁻ᶻ). Maps the real line smoothly onto (0, 1) with σ(0) = 0.5, turning raw scores into probabilities. Also one of the classic activation functions in neural networks.

decision-boundary.plain: The line where a classifier changes its mind — 50/50 territory between "yes" and "no".
decision-boundary.definition: The surface in feature-space where a classifier's predicted class flips. Linear models draw straight boundaries; trees draw axis-aligned boxes; kNN and neural networks can draw arbitrarily wiggly ones. A model's boundary shape IS its flexibility.

log-loss.plain: A grade for probability-speakers: pay by how little probability you gave the truth — confident wrongness costs dearly.
log-loss.definition: For true label y and predicted probability p̂: −[y·log(p̂) + (1−y)·log(1−p̂)], averaged over examples. Punishes overconfident mistakes hard, which trains honest uncertainty. The classification counterpart of mean squared error, and the loss behind most of deep learning.

knn.plain: Classify by asking the k most similar known examples to vote. No training — the dataset is the model.
knn.definition: A lazy learner: store all data; at query time, find the k closest points by distance and take the majority label (or average, for regression). Sensitive to feature scaling and slow at query time on big data — but simple, strong, and a perfect intuition pump.

decision-tree.plain: A flowchart of yes/no questions about the features, grown automatically — the rare model you can read.
decision-tree.definition: A model that recursively splits data with single-feature threshold questions, choosing each split to maximize class purity (e.g. minimize Gini impurity). Fully interpretable, trains fast, and overfits enthusiastically when grown deep — which is why it's usually deployed inside a forest.

random-forest.plain: Many deep trees, each trained on a random re-sample of the data, voting together — their private mistakes cancel, the shared pattern survives.
random-forest.definition: An ensemble of decision trees trained on bootstrap samples (and typically random feature subsets), predictions combined by majority vote or averaging. Trades the single tree's readability for markedly better generalization; a perennial best-first-try on tabular data.

ensemble.plain: A committee of models. Individually shaky, collectively steady — because their errors disagree and average away.
ensemble.definition: Any method combining multiple models' predictions (voting, averaging, stacking, boosting). Works exactly to the degree the members' errors are decorrelated — hence the deliberate randomness in how members are trained.

clustering.plain: Finding natural groups in data that nobody labeled — sorting the pile without being told the categories.
clustering.definition: The core unsupervised task: partition data points so members of a group are similar and groups are distinct. k-means is the classic algorithm; success is judged by group tightness/separation and usefulness, since no ground truth exists.

k-means.plain: Pick k guess-centers, then repeat two steps: every dot joins its nearest center; every center moves to the middle of its dots.
k-means.definition: A clustering algorithm minimizing total squared dot-to-center distance by alternating assignment and centroid-update steps until stable. Always converges, but to a local optimum — run several random restarts and keep the tightest. k is chosen by the human.

centroid.plain: A group's middle point: the plain average of its members, feature by feature.
centroid.definition: The component-wise mean of a set of vectors — the point minimizing total squared distance to the set, which is precisely why k-means' update step moves each center there.
